concise:
Ayah 1–19 · linked video: 02. Al-’Alaq (Ayah 1-19)

Surah Al-Alaq: The Opening Revelation

Ayah 1

ٱقْرَأْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ

The first words revealed to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ were “Iqra’ bismi rabbika alladhi khalaq” - Read in the name of your Master who created. This opening carries profound significance that unfolds on multiple levels.

The command to read came to someone who was ummi - unlettered. This word shares its root with umm (mother), suggesting someone as distant from formal education as a newborn child. It’s remarkable that “read” became the first commandment to a man who could not read, and to a people who were largely illiterate. The Prophet’s response ﷺ reflected this reality: “Ma ana bi-qari” - I am not one who can read.

Allah ﷻ chose the Arabian Peninsula - a place that, by worldly standards, was educationally behind the great civilizations of the time. The Persians had their legacy of philosophy and mathematics, the Romans their infrastructure and literature, the Chinese and Indians their scholarly traditions. The Arabs, by contrast, maintained primarily an oral tradition. Yet this apparent disadvantage was actually a strategic choice. These were people uncontaminated by complex philosophical systems, unmixed with mythologies that could distort the pure message. Their language was direct, rooted in concrete imagery rather than abstract speculation. Their fitrah - their natural disposition - remained largely uncorrupted, making them ideal recipients for a message that would be clearly distinct from all previous philosophies.

The phrase “bismi rabbika” - in the name of your Master - deserves careful attention. Allah ﷻ did not say “in the name of Allah,” though that name was well known among the Arabs. Instead, He emphasized the attribute of Rabb - Master. The Arabs knew the name Allah and would readily acknowledge Him as Creator of the heavens and earth. But recognizing Him as Rabb, as Master, meant acknowledging themselves as slaves. This was the critical shift in understanding.

The word Rabb encompasses multiple meanings: the Owner (Al-Malik), the Giver of gifts beyond necessity, the One in charge (As-Sayid), the Caretaker and Maintainer. When the Prophet ﷺ was told to read “in the name of your Master,” he was being assured: you are in good hands, under the care of One who owns you, provides for you, and maintains you. Don’t fear what’s coming. This isn’t just a command to read - it’s a command to read with a particular attitude, with the humility of a slave before his Master.

This positioning matters because the Quran speaks from authority. Allah ﷻ tells truth as it is, without apology, without sugarcoating. The one who comes to this Book must come recognizing the Master, not as a customer evaluating a product. We maintain the attitude of the slave while Allah ﷻ maintains the power of His word as Master. This is essential for the journey of revelation.

That the first word is “read” transforms our understanding of transformation itself. Before commandments about prayer or worship or seeking truth came the command to read. This suggests that at the heart of all change, all reformation, is education. The seed that would grow to transform the world in twenty-three years, that would reshape a region and eventually influence over a fifth of humanity, began with a single word: read.

This Book would spawn more scholarship, more libraries, more universities, more dedicated study than any other book in human history. The essence of transformation is reading the word of Allah ﷻ with the right attitude.

The verse continues: “alladhi khalaq” - who created. Allah ﷻ left this open-ended. Created what? The heavens, the earth, the angels, all of creation. The imagination runs through the vastness of what He has created. But this openness prepares us for the specificity that follows.

Ayah 2

خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ

After opening the scope of Allah’s creation to encompass everything, He narrows it: He created the human being from ’alaq. Of all that He created, first and foremost, think about what has been created in terms of yourself.

The word ’alaq shares its root with words meaning to cling, to be stuck, to hang on. It describes the embryo in its earliest stage, literally clinging to the uterine wall, suspended like a leech that latches on and doesn’t let go. ’Alaq can also mean a leech itself - that creature that clings and draws sustenance from what it attaches to.

To us, anything that hangs and feeds parasitically off something else appears disgusting or pathetic, helpless and dependent. This is the reference Allah ﷻ makes to our physical origin. Other places in the Quran mention the nutfah (sperm drop), but here there’s a literary continuity. Earlier passages referenced the creation of male and female, and now Allah ﷻ moves forward in that sequence to reference pregnancy itself. He created you by way of this clinging, dependent state.

This grounds the human being in humility. Before we’re told about our dignity and nobility, we’re reminded of our origin: we were once completely helpless, entirely dependent, clinging to life itself.

Ayah 3

ٱقْرَأْ وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْأَكْرَمُ

The command returns: “Iqra’ wa rabbuka al-akram” - Read, and your Master is the Most Noble, the Most Generous, the Most Dignified.

The second command to read has sparked various interpretations. Some say the first “read” was for personal benefit, the second for teaching others. Others suggested the first was for learning, the second for instruction. Some distinguished between reading in prayer and outside it. But perhaps the simplest understanding is that this is how natural, powerful speech works. When giving a strong command to someone reluctant, you repeat yourself: Read. Read. This isn’t textual speech - it’s conversational, urgent, encouraging someone who hesitates because he doesn’t know what to do.

Now we encounter one of the unique names of Allah ﷻ in the Quran: Al-Akram - the Most Noble, the Most Generous. The word karim appears throughout the Quran describing various things: noble angels (kiram bararah), a generous provision (rizq karim), the Quran itself (Quran karim). But the superlative Al-Akram - the Most Noble - appears only twice in the entire Quran. Once here, and once when Allah ﷻ says “inna akramakum ’indallahi atqakum” - the most noble among you in Allah’s sight are those with the most taqwa. That usage limits the superlative to nobility among people. This is the only place where Al-Akram is used exclusively for Allah ﷻ.

This is part of the nobility of these opening passages themselves - that they contain a name of Allah ﷻ used nowhere else in the Quran.

What does it mean that our Master is the Most Noble, the Most Generous? Every name of Allah ﷻ impacts the slave in two ways. When Allah ﷻ is merciful, we hope for His mercy and we emanate mercy ourselves. When He is forgiving, we seek His forgiveness and we become forgiving. When He is the Most Dignified, we hope He dignifies us, and we become people who dignify others.

But there’s something deeper here. Think about prestige in education. Someone pays premium tuition to attend Harvard or another elite institution. Just the name - that you studied there - becomes part of your dignity, regardless of your grades. If a researcher studies under the leading expert in their field, that becomes an honor. In the religious world, studying under a renowned scholar or receiving ijazah from someone with the highest chain of transmission becomes a source of prestige.

Education has historically been one of the most lasting forms of prestige. Rulers come and go, their power fades. But teachers and people of knowledge pass down their contributions generation after generation. We know who Plato was; we don’t know who ruled Athens in his time. Muslims know Imam Bukhari (RA) but not necessarily who the Khalifa was during his era. Knowledge gave a prestige that outlasted everything else.

Your prestige in education comes from who your teacher was, which institution you attended. Now consider: when you become a student of the Quran, who have you accepted as your teacher? You’ve accepted Allah ﷻ Himself as your teacher. You couldn’t have a more dignified teacher, which means you couldn’t have a more dignified honor for yourself. To be a student of Allah’s own word - this is the implication of reading as your Master is the Most Noble.

This transforms how we approach Quran recitation and study. Whether or not people give us dignity, if we come to Allah’s word with humility, seeking that He dignify us, He will open that door. Our time spent reciting Quran becomes one of the most dignifying, most prestigious acts we can engage in. This is how Allah ﷻ has honored us through His word.

Some scholars also connected karim to its meaning of generosity - that Allah ﷻ will open doors of forgiveness and cover sins as we recite His word. Others noted that while the Prophet ﷺ has been dignified, we must remember Allah ﷻ is the Most Dignified. Still others tied it to never losing hope in Allah ﷻ, since He keeps giving favors. Our study of the Quran becomes an attempt to continuously re-inspire ourselves with hope in Him. And some connected it to the hadith that every letter of the Quran we recite earns ten good deeds - that’s the generosity (karam) of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 4

ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَ بِٱلْقَلَمِ

“Alladhi ’allama bil-qalam” - Who taught by the pen. This isn’t a separate statement but a continuation, a description of Al-Akram. Part of what makes Allah ﷻ the Most Noble is that He became a teacher, that He took the role of teacher, and specifically, He taught by means of the pen.

This is significant because many scholars consider this the first revelation, while Surah Al-Qalam (The Pen) - “Nun, wal-qalami wa ma yasturun” - was likely the second revelation. Both the first and second revelations to the Prophet ﷺ reference the pen. This demands we ask: what is this pen?

Some scholars tie it to the angels who are scribes, who have access to the highest revelation, who write it down. Perhaps this references those noble, recording angels (kiram bararah) who were mentioned in earlier passages. The process of delivering Allah’s knowledge to humanity began with the pen of the angels.

Others see the pen as referring to the documentation of revelation itself - how revelation is preserved with Allah ﷻ and delivered down to us.

But Allah ﷻ left the matter open-ended. He decided to teach humanity by means of the pen. Since reading has been mentioned, pen naturally follows - when you read something, obviously you’re reading what has been written. The mention of the pen takes the Prophet ﷺ back through the entire journey of human knowledge and history, suggesting: I have been educating humanity through their writing of books, through their producing and archiving knowledge. This journey of reading and learning across societies and civilizations is something I gave them. They didn’t develop this on their own.

This connects to a critical teaching: certain things are heavenly gifts, not products of human evolution. Some people might not immediately recognize as divine gifts include:

First, clothing. Allah ﷻ says in the Quran that He sent down clothing for us. The urge to clothe ourselves is actually a heavenly gift, not merely social evolution.

Second, language itself. “Ar-Rahman, ’allama al-Quran, khalaqa al-insan, ’allamahu al-bayan” - He taught the Quran, created the human being, taught him clear expression. Our ability to speak and communicate is a divine gift, not just an evolutionary development. The speech center in our brain, our ability to put words together and articulate them - Allah ﷻ inspired this into us.

Third, writing. We can archive what we speak, put thought to pen, document it. This too is given by Allah ﷻ.

All of this comes together in this passage, just as it does in Surah Ar-Rahman. We were given the ability to speak, the ability to write, the ability to produce remarkable knowledge and literature. But we were given all of this so that one day we could read what Allah ﷻ had the angels write for us, what He had transcribed. One day we could reach our ultimate purpose in learning to speak, read and write: so that we could come into contact with the final words of Allah ﷻ, the final message. The Prophet ﷺ is being told: this is the climax of Allah’s gift. This is the highest point that can be reached.

The Book called the Quran - Quran itself means recitation - suggests that the experience of true reading, true recitation, hasn’t been fully experienced until one engages with the Quran. This is the height of reading itself.

Ayah 5

عَلَّمَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ

“’Allama al-insana ma lam ya’lam” - He taught the human being what he did not know, what he could not have known.

Notice the repetition building through these verses: khalaq, khalaq (created, created), iqra’, iqra’ (read, read), ’allama, ’allama (taught, taught). The words repeat themselves, and the repeated words all relate to the same themes - creation, reading, teaching, knowledge.

This verse captures two dimensions of knowledge that humanity couldn’t access without the pen. First, human knowledge: if previous generations hadn’t written down what they knew, the next generation couldn’t build upon it. Without the archives of history, the writings of people of the past, we couldn’t have built knowledge layer upon layer.

Consider how education works today. Students read textbooks that are constantly updated with new research, building on existing knowledge. One generation creates a foundation, the next builds upon it, then the next takes it further. We call them “next generation technologies” - each generation of technology builds on what came before. Without the pen, without archiving, none of this would exist. The medicine we have, the science we practice, the technology we use - none of it would be here if centuries-old materials weren’t accessible to each successive generation.

That’s one side - human knowledge. The other side is divine knowledge. Allah ﷻ taught by the pen of the heavens that delivers revelation. This is knowledge we could have absolutely no access to otherwise.

By combining both dimensions, Allah ﷻ highlights something crucial: knowledge itself is sacred. All knowledge is taught by Allah ﷻ. The pen itself is sacred. When someone says “I want to study deen, not dunya,” this creates a false separation. When you study physics, biology, history - whatever field - you’re also studying what Allah ﷻ taught, what He allowed to be archived. When you study Allah’s word, you’re doing the same. These two worlds must live together.

You cannot study the Quran and not know history, not know the world around you. The Book itself forces you to look around. It keeps telling you to observe, to investigate. “Look at how creation began” - this is the Quran telling you to go find the origin of species. Are you going to find that sitting in an Islamic library? No, you’ll have to explore, to examine archaeological sites. This is the call for exploration, for seeking knowledge in all its forms. Both worlds of knowledge come together in service of understanding Allah’s revelation and His creation.

Ayah 6

كَلَّآ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَيَطْغَىٰٓ

“Kalla inna al-insana layatgha” - No! Not at all! Indeed, the human being transgresses, rebels.

This is now the second passage of the surah, and understanding its coherence with what came before sheds light on the historical context. Remember what Waraqah ibn Nawfal told the Prophet ﷺ: “I wish I had the strength of youth, because the time is coming when your people will expel you, will be harsh toward you.” The Prophet ﷺ was shocked: “Them, toward me?”

Also remember why the Prophet ﷺ went to the cave in the first place - he saw crime in society, terrible things happening. Now Allah ﷻ tells him: I have taught you what you couldn’t have known, I’ve taught humanity what they couldn’t have known. But the reality is: “Kalla” - on the contrary, in balance - “inna al-insana layatgha” - without doubt, the human being has been rebellious, truly has been rebellious.

This is Allah ﷻ confirming the Prophet’s observation. Yes, I know. I know what you’ve experienced. I’m telling you this knowledge I’m giving you is the answer. The crime of humanity, the question it raised - that’s what brought you here. So first Allah ﷻ acknowledges the observation of the Prophet ﷺ.

The word tughyan is used when water exceeds its bounds, when there’s a flood. Human beings exceed their bounds, cross lines, trample over other people’s rights and security. They say what shouldn’t be said, do what shouldn’t be done. Allah ﷻ then provides a diagnosis, an explanation.

Ayah 7

أَن رَّءَاهُ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰٓ

“An ra’ahu istaghna” - Because he sees himself as self-sufficient, independent, not needing anyone.

This is why. All the crimes the human being commits stem from thinking he can get away with it. The strong survive, might makes right, he’s not dependent on anybody, nobody can question him, nobody can come after him. If I can get away with it, I will.

Think of being at a red light with no cop around, no camera. You look carefully both ways. What do you do? When you think you can’t be caught, when you feel there’s nobody over you, when you believe you’re completely independent and will get away with it - then you’ll just do it.

There’s a profound passage in the Quran where Allah ﷻ says that if He opened the doors of provisions for His slaves completely - if people had no money problems, no food problems, no shelter problems, if they had everything they needed - then there would be great crimes in the world. If people truly became independent, having no needs at all, they would do whatever they want without concern for consequences.

Allah ﷻ is saying: people who think they’re independent, people who think there are no consequences, they commit these crimes. That’s the root cause of the criminal behavior you’ve witnessed.

Ayah 8

إِنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ٱلرُّجْعَىٰٓ

“Inna ila rabbika ar-ruj’a” - Indeed, to your Master alone is the final return.

Now Allah ﷻ gives the solution. First, He stated the problem (human transgression). Then He gave the root of the problem (seeing themselves as independent). Now He provides the solution: internalize that you’re going to return to Allah ﷻ and answer for what you did. If a person can truly internalize this - that they have to go back to Allah ﷻ - their behavior changes. Their speech changes. Their lifestyle changes.

Until that’s internalized, that they have to return, crime will continue. Until you establish in people that they must go back to Allah ﷻ, crime won’t stop.

Consider a powerful historical comparison. In 1920s America, Prohibition was enacted - alcohol was banned nationwide. What happened? More alcohol was brewed in those years than in any previous period of American history. People were making it in their basements, getting sick from moonshine. The regulation couldn’t be enforced, it broke, and we see where society is now.

Compare this to an incident in the Prophet’s life ﷺ. Revelation came that alcohol was now prohibited. For many people, wine was actually their drink of choice - they didn’t drink water as much as they drank wine, and this was in the desert where fluids were scarce. When this ayah came down, there was no army, no military police, no enforcement, no tax penalties, no fines, no jail time, no punishment structure whatsoever. Just the word of Allah ﷻ. Nothing more.

The narrations tell us rivers were flowing with wine. The Companions (RA) who heard that revelation had come started gagging themselves to throw it up. Compare the American prohibition of alcohol to the Islamic prohibition of alcohol - just the word of Allah ﷻ, nothing else. No army, no police, no enforcement, no penalties. Yet look at what these people did. They gagged themselves, threw up what they’d consumed, worried they may have taken something Allah ﷻ doesn’t want.

Why? Because it’s not “I don’t see any cops” - it’s “In the end, I have to go back to Him.” When that’s internalized, whether somebody’s watching or not, whether there’s enforcement or not: I’m in trouble with Him, I won’t do it. They spilled it all out. And it wasn’t just that it was prohibited - Allah ﷻ said it’s rijs min ’amal ash-shaytan - an abomination from the work of the devil. That’s why they didn’t just stop drinking it; they spilled it all, got rid of it completely. They didn’t want the devil in their houses. They were gagging themselves because “I have something of the devil’s work inside my body.”

This is what we need to understand about “inna ila rabbika ar-ruj’a.” When that belief - that ultimate return - is truly internalized, behavior transforms even without external enforcement.

Ayah 9-10

أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يَنْهَىٰ • عَبْدًا إِذَا صَلَّىٰٓ

“Ara’ayta alladhi yanha ’abdan idha salla” - Have you seen the one who forbids a slave when he prays?

This is now the observation of the Prophet ﷺ up to this point. But now that he’s become a messenger, now that he has the word of Allah ﷻ and has been commanded to recite it, he needs to be mentally prepared. When he goes back and recites this, there will be people who find it agitating. They won’t like that he’s reciting it.

It’s not just that he’d seen criminal behavior before in people who don’t think they’ll return to Allah ﷻ - now he’ll see things happening toward him. And Allah ﷻ left it somewhat mysterious: “Have you seen the one who prevents a slave when he prays?” The slave is the Messenger himself, but Allah ﷻ made it general, didn’t say “prevents you.” He said: “a slave when he prays.”

This happened because Abu Jahl had announced: if anybody sees Muhammad praying, they should beat him up. There’s a narration that he literally said if he sees the Prophet ﷺ prostrating before his Lord, reciting Quran and praying, he’s going to step on his neck himself - because the head will be down anyway.

Some scholars distinguish between narrations and connect this to the incident at Ta’if, which occurred ten years into the mission. Up until then, the Prophet ﷺ could be harassed, but after Ta’if - where he was stoned, ridiculed, tortured, covered in blood - something changed. For the first time, he complained to Allah ﷻ about how weak he’d become. Then he said: “If You’re pleased with me, I don’t care.” Angels came and made an offer: Allah ﷻ is ready to crush this city between its two mountains if you give the word.

That was the first time angels came with an offer of retaliation rather than Allah ﷻ simply telling the Prophet ﷺ to be patient and ignore them. Some scholars argue this was the turning point in the seerah. From then on, Allah ﷻ did not allow His Prophet ﷺ to be physically tortured. Things changed.

Before this incident, if he prayed, they threw trash on him, dead animal skins, even beat him up. After this incident, such things stopped happening. Abu Jahl had said “If I see him praying, I’ll step on his neck.” So he sees the Prophet ﷺ praying and thinks “I gave my word, I’m a thug, I’m going to do this.” He starts walking toward the Prophet ﷺ, and suddenly he starts gesturing wildly in thin air. People asked “What are you doing?” He said: “As I approached this man, I saw a huge canyon gap open up between us, filled with fire, and winged creatures were reaching to grab me and pull me down.”

He wasn’t going to mess with that anymore. We’ll see references corroborating this later in the surah.

But now the Prophet ﷺ is being mentally prepared: there will be crimes against you even as you pray. Waraqah ibn Nawfal (RA) told him people would expel him. The first step toward that would be what? They won’t even let you pray. They’d never stopped him from anything before, but now he’s being prepared for this escalation.

Ayah 11-12

أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَانَ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ • أَوْ أَمَرَ بِٱلتَّقْوَىٰٓ

“Ara’ayta in kana ’ala al-huda, aw amara bit-taqwa” - Have you considered if he had been upon guidance, or commanded to righteousness?

Now Allah ﷻ makes a profound comment about Abu Jahl, and we need to understand something about him. Compare him with Abu Lahab - those two famous villains of Makkah, both uncles of the Prophet ﷺ by relation.

Abu Lahab was basically one of the greediest, angriest, cheapest, most despicable men in Makkah. People were afraid to deal with him in any capacity. He was known for a vicious temper, for insulting people, for being ruthless. He was in charge of the treasury and was known for stealing from it. He had quite a record.

Abu Jahl, on the other hand, was an arrogant man, yes, but also a noble man. He was well-respected. His original name was actually Abu al-Hakam - the father of wisdom. We named him Abu Jahl (father of ignorance) sarcastically because of his denial of Islam. But he was actually a wise person who made good decisions for the city. He had a lot of pride, but he wasn’t as horrible as Abu Lahab.

The Prophet ﷺ saw this. He recognized this difference in Abu Jahl. That’s why he prayed: “Allahumma a’izz al-Islam bi-ahad al-’Umarayn” - O Allah, give me one of the two Umars - meaning Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) or Amr ibn Hisham (Abu Jahl). Allah ﷻ gave him one - Umar (RA). But the fact that the Prophet ﷺ prayed for one of these two shows he saw potential good in Abu Jahl.

And Allah ﷻ saw all that good in him too. Allah ﷻ saw the potential. Yes, this person will try to stop you from praying, but: “Have you seen if he had become committed to guidance?” That kind of person who’s even willing to stop you from prayer - he could have been the other way. He could have been someone committed to guidance. “Aw amara bit-taqwa” - He could have been someone who commands others to righteousness, a proactive supporter of the cause of Islam. He could have been just as forceful for Islam, for the work of the Prophet ﷺ, as he ended up being against it.

This reminds me of someone I met in Dubai, originally from Switzerland. You may remember the minaret controversy in Switzerland years ago, when they tried to ban minarets on mosques. One politician was at the forefront of that controversy, speaking ill of the Prophet ﷺ, saying vile things about Islam. He was one of the most outright anti-Muslim bigots out there.

When I met him in Dubai, he had taken shahada. He brought his non-Muslim son, who also took shahada there. He apologized for what he’d said about the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. And he was actually campaigning to teach people about the Prophet ﷺ. He could have been Abu Jahl, but then he became like Umar (RA) - just flipped, switched sides.

Notice here the words huda and taqwa. These connect back to earlier in the surah. All of that revelation - iqra’ bismi rabbik, ’allama bil-qalam, ’allama al-insana ma lam ya’lam - all of that is huda, guidance. And when a person thinks of themselves as istaghna - independent, not caring, having no concern - the opposite of that is taqwa. So there’s huda and taqwa here, while previously we had istaghna and tughyan - the opposites of taqwa.

Ayah 13

أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ

“Ara’ayta in kadhdhaba wa tawalla” - Have you seen if he denies and turns away?

What if he stays on this course? If he calls it all a lie and turns his back? These are the two things accomplished by someone who campaigns against truth: kadhdhaba - to call the messenger a liar, to call the Quran a lie, to convince others he’s a liar; and tawalla - to turn back himself, to not care for it personally.

These words actually summarize the entire passage. In the beginning, someone who rebels and doesn’t care (yatgha, istaghna) is someone who has turned away. Someone who is stopping you from praying, who is hurting you - that’s the one campaigning against the truth. “In kadhdhaba wa tawalla” captures both dimensions.

Ayah 14

أَلَمْ يَعْلَم بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرَىٰ

“Alam ya’lam bi-anna Allaha yara” - Does he not know that Allah ﷻ sees?

Notice the progression through this passage. Allah ﷻ said “an ra’ahu istaghna” - he sees himself as independent. Then: “Ara’ayta alladhi yanha” - Do you see the one who prevents? “Ara’ayta in kana ’ala al-huda” - Do you see if he was on guidance? “Ara’ayta in kadhdhaba” - Do you see if he turned back?

Seeing, seeing, seeing, seeing. And how does it all end? “Doesn’t he know that Allah ﷻ sees?” So beautiful - all of that “Do you observe? Does he see himself?” And in all of that, does he forget that Allah ﷻ is the One who sees? The One who observes everything?

Ayah 15-16

كَلَّا لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ لَنَسْفَعًۢا بِٱلنَّاصِيَةِ • نَاصِيَةٍ كَـٰذِبَةٍ خَاطِئَةٍ

“Kalla la’in lam yantahi la-nasfa’an bin-nasiyah, nasiyatin kadhibatin khati’ah” - No! If he does not desist, We will surely drag him by the forelock - a lying, sinning forelock.

Kalla - No! Not at all! If he doesn’t stop it, if he doesn’t quit: “la-nasfa’an bin-nasiyah” - We are going to grab him by the front, by the nasiyah (forelock).

When someone’s nasiyah is grabbed - the front of the head - they’re not in combat with you. They’re captured as a slave. Their hands are tied behind their back, they’re on their knees, and the captor comes and grabs their head like that - like a prisoner, humbled, someone who no longer has free motion. In combat or fighting, you don’t go for someone’s head like that - it’s one of the most defended areas. They’ll block you, they’ll hit back. But if someone is completely disarmed, then you can grab them by the hair. It’s a position of complete subjugation.

“If he doesn’t stop, We’re going to grab him by his nasiyah” - “nasiyatin kadhibatin khati’ah” - this nasiyah, this forehead, that lies and makes mistakes. Allah ﷻ is referring to the mind, basically. This person doesn’t use his mind properly, and that’s why he’s going to pay.

It’s remarkable that Allah ﷻ makes reference to the forehead that lies, that makes wrong calculations. Go back to the beginning: Allah ﷻ said read, learn. He’ll teach you what you don’t know. When you read and learn, you learn to use your mind. You accept truth. But if you refuse to learn, if you think you don’t need it, then you’re not going to use your mind. And if you don’t, then there are better uses for your head - it’s only good for getting grabbed on Judgment Day.

Ayah 17-18

فَلْيَدْعُ نَادِيَهُۥ • سَنَدْعُ ٱلزَّبَانِيَةَ

“Fal-yad’u nadiyah, sanad’u az-zabaniyah” - So let him call his associates. We will call the angels of Hell.

Allah ﷻ opens a challenge. Remember that Waraqah (RA) told the Prophet ﷺ: your people are going to expel you. Now Allah ﷻ says: let him call his cronies, his people. Nadi means the people you call upon.

This ties to an incident. The Prophet ﷺ was standing by the Kaaba, and Abu Jahl passed by. He said: “Didn’t I stop you from doing this? Didn’t I tell you not to pray here anymore?” He started threatening the Prophet ﷺ. And the Prophet ﷺ responded harshly: “Leave me alone!”

Abu Jahl said: “What do you have? What army do you have that you’re going to threaten me with? What do you think you are? I swear by Allah, I have this entire valley - they’ll come for me and beat you to death if I want them to.”

Allah ﷻ responded. The Prophet ﷺ recited in his face: “Fal-yad’u nadiyah” - Let him call the people he’s going to call. “Sanad’u az-zabaniyah” - We’ll call the angels.

Zabaniyah is an interesting word. It comes from daf’ and shatm - a strong force that protects but also pushes back and can kill. In modern terms, it would be like anti-riot police with shields who form a defensive line but also go on the offensive, killing the enemy as part of their defense. Allah ﷻ says: you want to call yours? I’ll call Mine.

Imagine this scene. The Prophet ﷺ standing by himself in front of the biggest thugs in the city, the most wealthy and powerful man who says “I’m going to call everybody and we’ll beat you to death, we’ll kill you.” And the Prophet ﷺ, reciting the word of Allah ﷻ, responds: “Call them. We’ll call Allah’s defensive and attacking force.”

The voice is that of the Prophet ﷺ, but the words are those of Allah ﷻ. Can you imagine the courage required? How politically incorrect this is? How much he’s throwing himself into harm’s way? Yet this is evidence that he is a messenger of Allah ﷻ. Why would anybody say the kinds of things that could get them killed unless they were delivering a message from the Master?

The Quran wasn’t recited in isolation. It was recited and these people - the very ones it was talking about - heard it. They heard it in their faces. The Prophet ﷺ is one man by himself. “’Abdan idha salla” - there’s nobody even praying with him, he’s alone. Yet he’s speaking in this authoritative tone. Because yes, the slave of Allah ﷻ is a slave. But the word of Allah ﷻ is the word of the Master. The slave is reciting the word of the Master.

There’s a contrast here we must maintain. We maintain the humility of a slave, but Allah ﷻ maintains the power of His word as the Master as it comes through us. We can’t develop arrogance because we’re reciting Allah’s word. We must maintain our humility as we recite.

Ayah 19

كَلَّا لَا تُطِعْهُ وَٱسْجُدْ وَٱقْتَرِب ۩

“Kalla la tuti’hu wasjud waqtarib” - No! Do not obey him, but prostrate and draw near.

“Kalla la tuti’hu” - Don’t just obey him. La tuti’ means both “don’t obey” and “don’t pay attention” - here it means don’t even bother with him. Don’t pay attention to this guy. Ignore him.

One of the most influential, most VIP people in Makkah, and Allah ﷻ says: Don’t bother. Not at all.

“Wasjud” - And prostrate. Remember the threat? “If you make sajdah, I’ll step on your neck.” Allah ﷻ says: No, go ahead. Make sajdah. Let’s see him try.

But understand what’s happened by Allah ﷻ saying this. The Prophet ﷺ is cutting himself off from the people most influential in society. Society is all about networking - who you know, who you’re close to, who you’re far from. By saying this in the Quran, he’s cut himself off from Abu Jahl and anyone associated with him. Anybody seen close to the Prophet ﷺ will be thought of as an enemy. Anyone who wants to get close to him becomes socially toxic. People will stay away, not only because they don’t want to hear his message, but because now they’ll get in trouble with Abu Jahl. Just keep your distance. Nobody wants to get close to you.

You’re no longer close to these people of influence and protection. You’ve been isolated.

How does the surah end? “Waqtarib” - And get close. Draw near.

The only closeness you need is closeness to Allah ﷻ. Don’t worry about anything else. What an ending. “Don’t pay attention to him. Make sajdah and get the connection that is stronger than any other connection” - get close to Allah ﷻ.

I’m reminded of another passage we heard in salah today, from the end of Surah Al-Hijr: “We know that your chest is constricted because of what they say. So declare the perfection of your Master and be from those who make sajdah. And keep worshiping your Master until death comes to you.”

When we make sajdah, we say “subhana rabbiya al-a’la” - Glory to my Master, the Most High. “Declare the perfection of your Master” connects to “make sajdah.” And when death came to the Prophet ﷺ, he was the closest to Allah ﷻ. His final words were “Ar-rafiq al-a’la” - he wanted to go to the highest companion, to be close with Allah ﷻ again.

Here: make sajdah and get close. Before death even comes, when we make sajdah before Allah ﷻ, we’re getting closer and closer to Him. It’s Allah’s way of telling us that sajdah is when the slave gets closest to Allah ﷻ. The most humble position is the highest place you will reach spiritually. Physically you’re at your lowest, spiritually you’re at your highest. That’s the power of sajdah.

Coherence of the Surah

The coherence of this surah is remarkable. So far in my study of the Quran, I’ve only found one other surah with a similar structure - Surah Maryam. That surah presents seven different passages, then concludes with seven ayahs, and each of those final ayahs summarizes one of the earlier passages.

Here in Surah Al-Alaq, there are two fundamental passages. The first is about revelation itself. The second is about the criminal and his behavior.

Review this in your mind:

First passage (revelation): Read in the name of your Master who created. Created the human being from a clinging clot. Read, and your Master is the Most Noble, who taught by the pen, who taught the human being what he didn’t know.

Second passage (the criminal): The human being rebels, sees himself as independent, will be returned to Allah ﷻ. Did you see the one who prevents a slave from praying? What if he had been on guidance? What if he had commanded to good? Did you see the one who denied and turned away? Doesn’t he know Allah ﷻ sees? If he doesn’t stop, We’ll grab him by the forelock - that lying, sinning forelock. Let him call his cronies, We’ll call Our forces.

Then the final ayah covers both: Don’t pay attention to him - make sajdah and draw near.

Two passages, and two necessary reactions:

The first passage was about revelation. What should it lead to? Make sajdah and draw near to Allah ﷻ. That’s the point of revelation - it should bring you to the kind of humility that draws you close before Allah ﷻ.

The second passage was about this criminal and his escalating behavior. What should you do? Don’t pay attention to him. Let him do what he does. Just know that Allah ﷻ knows exactly what he’s doing. He’s going to return to Allah ﷻ anyway. Don’t worry - I’ll deal with him.

This sets the tone for the Prophet ﷺ to do his work, because as he does it, opposition will intensify and escalate. Allah ﷻ tells him: You want security when things get worse? You want connections when things get worse? Here’s your connection - make sajdah and get closer to Allah ﷻ.
